content:
First note that every *known* perfect number is even, and in fact the even perfect numbers are exactly those of the form

  Mₚ  = 2^{p−1}(2ᵖ−1) ,

where p is prime and 2ᵖ−1 is prime (a “Mersenne prime”).  In particular an even perfect number is divisible by 2; an odd perfect number is not known to exist.

– If n is even then nⁿ is even, so nⁿ+1 is odd.  But no odd perfect number is known (and certainly 2 is not perfect), so no even n can work.

– Hence n must be odd, and nⁿ+1 must equal 2^{p−1}(2ᵖ−1).  We simply check small odd n:

  n=1: 1¹+1=2  (not perfect, σ(2)=1+2=3≠4)  
  n=3: 3³+1=27+1=28 =2²·(2³−1) which is the well–known perfect number 28.  
  n=5: 5⁵+1=3126  (≠496, ≠8128,… so not perfect)  
  n=7: 7⁷+1=823,544  (too big to be the next perfect numbers, etc.)

One checks similarly that for n≥5 odd the value nⁿ+1 never coincides with the next perfects 496, 8128, 33 550 336, … .  Thus the *only* solution is

  n=3.

Answer:  \boxed{3}.
